text: The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to begin dissection at the mid portion of the stomach, progressing towards the upper portion of the fundus, with grasping arms positioned on either side of the working arm.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to pull up and the first arm to pull down and laterally, exposing the left crus and the angle of His. The arms are then repositioned, with the fourth arm placed lower and the first arm higher, allowing the mobilization of the fat pad off the angle of His and facilitating the descent of the fundus.

The surgeon uses the first robotic arm positioned lower to navigate through filmy adhesions attached to the pancreas, addressing the challenge of a less decompressed fundus during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to splay the stomach and lift it up and laterally, revealing a distinct dimple in the fat. This visual cue guides the dissection into an avascular plane, allowing the surgeon to separate fatty tissue from the posterior aspect of the fundus and slide up towards the left crus.

The surgeon uses wristed instruments to complete the exposure of the left crus and mobilize the angle of His. The fourth arm is brought down to grasp the large fat pad, ensuring it is completely freed up. This maneuver helps maintain tension off the staple line, preventing it from tucking underneath the left crus.
The surgeon inspects the area near the gastroesophageal junction and the base of the left crus, ensuring all tissue is cleared to prevent tension on the staple line and avoid a retained fundus.

The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach, using the E-one-hundred generator to seal vessels efficiently. The dissection progresses towards the pylorus, with visible manipulation of the stomach's varying shapes.
The surgeon checks the mobilization of the stomach near the pylorus, estimating the distance to be approximately four centimeters, while ensuring the tissue is adequately freed.

The surgeon moves the camera to the right trocar near the umbilicus. A 40 French bougie is inserted, and the SureForm stapler with a green load is prepared for use, facilitating precise stapling without manual adjustments.

The surgeon slides the stapler behind the specimen side of the stomach, lifting it up and laterally to position the stapler. The stapler is fired up towards the angle of His, staying just lateral to it, ensuring the staple line does not extend onto the esophagus. The rim of tissue being stapled is visibly small.

The surgeon places the gastric specimen to the side and begins suturing the staple line using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture. The initial throws imbricate the corner of the staple line, followed by sewing the omentum back to the staple line.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to sew the omentum to the staple line, carefully grasping the cut edge of the omentum and bringing it over to the staple line. Multiple suture throws are made to secure the omentum, with the final throw locked to enhance hemostasis at the corner of the staple line.
